coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical N2-to-NH3 with high faradaic efficiency on ZrO2/carbon quantum dots catalysts in neutral electrolyte

coredata.dc:description: Electrochemical nitrogen reduction (NRR) to synthesize NH3 is limited by low Faraday efficiency (FE). Developing electrocatalysts that can efficiently adsorb/activate N2 and suppress hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) to enhance FE is needed. In this work, x-ZrO2/CQDs-T series catalysts were successfully prepared. The results demonstrate that: (I) The appropriate introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) effectively enhances the surface adsorption capacity of ZrO2/CQDs for N2. (II) By high-temperature calcination eliminates the alkaline functional group (-NH2) of CQDs, which reduces the basicity of ZrO2/CQDs catalyst and inhibits the adsorption of protons on the catalyst surface. (III) Due to the semiconductor properties of ZrO2, the charge transfer efficiency of ZrO2/CQDs catalyst is significantly reduced, thereby weakening the binding of electrons to the adsorbed protons. As a result, 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst appropriately and effectually restricts the accessibility of protons/electrons and promotes more N2 molecules to occupy active sites, thus achieving up to 66.62 % FE, which is superior to most reported NRR catalysts so far. Meanwhile, electrochemical in situ ATR-SEIRAS technique reveals that 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst surface occurs in associative alternating reactions. The introduction of CQDs not only effectively affects the adsorption of N2 and N-related intermediates on ZrO2/CQDs catalyst and limits the proton/electron accessibility to improve FE of NRR, but also promotes the exposure of active sites to increase NH3 yield.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical N2-to-NH3 with high faradaic efficiency on ZrO2/carbon quantum dots catalysts in neutral electrolyte

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology, School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology, School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrochemical nitrogen reduction (NRR) to synthesize NH3 is limited by low Faraday efficiency (FE). Developing electrocatalysts that can efficiently adsorb/activate N2 and suppress hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) to enhance FE is needed. In this work, x-ZrO2/CQDs-T series catalysts were successfully prepared. The results demonstrate that: (I) The appropriate introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) effectively enhances the surface adsorption capacity of ZrO2/CQDs for N2. (II) By high-temperature calcination eliminates the alkaline functional group (-NH2) of CQDs, which reduces the basicity of ZrO2/CQDs catalyst and inhibits the adsorption of protons on the catalyst surface. (III) Due to the semiconductor properties of ZrO2, the charge transfer efficiency of ZrO2/CQDs catalyst is significantly reduced, thereby weakening the binding of electrons to the adsorbed protons. As a result, 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst appropriately and effectually restricts the accessibility of protons/electrons and promotes more N2 molecules to occupy active sites, thus achieving up to 66.62 % FE, which is superior to most reported NRR catalysts so far. Meanwhile, electrochemical in situ ATR-SEIRAS technique reveals that 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst surface occurs in associative alternating reactions. The introduction of CQDs not only effectively affects the adsorption of N2 and N-related intermediates on ZrO2/CQDs catalyst and limits the proton/electron accessibility to improve FE of NRR, but also promotes the exposure of active sites to increase NH3 yield.

ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia (NH3), as one of the most important chemicals in modern civilization, is an essential component in agricultural and chemical industrial production [1–3]. Moreover, it is also a hydrogen energy carrier with excellent application prospects [4,5]. Currently, industrial synthesis of ammonia mainly relies on the traditional Haber-Bosch process [6]. However, the process of ammonia production by this technology not only consumes a large amount of fossil energy but also releases large amounts of CO2 [7,8]. Therefore, exploring a more cost-effective and environment-friendly method for ammonia production is urgently needed, such as electrochemical method, photocatalysis method, and nitrogenase method, etc. [9–11].

In recent years, with the development of electrochemical technology, electrochemical nitrogen reduction (NRR) to NH3 has attracted much attention, which uses water as a proton source and relies on sustainable power sources to provide the necessary electricity, such as solar and wind energy [12,13]. From the perspective of thermodynamics, although NRR process is favorable at room temperature, experimental results often exhibit low selectivity [14], which is mainly due to the competitive hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and lower N2 adsorption capacity on catalyst surface [15–19]. Therefore, developing high-performance NRR electrocatalysts that can efficiently adsorb N2 and suppress H adsorption is essential for significantly improving FE of NRR.

In order to improve FE for NRR to NH3, various efficient catalysts have been developed, such as precious metal electrocatalysts (Au [20], Rh [21], Pd [22], Ag [23], Pt [24]), which have demonstrated excellent catalytic performance for NRR. However, its scarcity and high-cost limit its extensive industrial application. Therefore, the development of more economical, low-cost and abundant transition metal oxides (TMO) as NRR electrocatalysts has attracted more attention, including Fe3O4 [25], NiO [26], CuO [27], Co3O4 [28] and Fe2O3 [29] and so on. It is reported that many TMO-based NRR electrocatalysts can easily form metal-H bonds to achieve efficient HER reaction, resulting in low FE of NH3. However, through experiments and calculations, Tao et al. demonstrated that Zr prefers to bind to N rather than H, which indicates that Zr-based catalysts may be beneficial to preferentially generate NH3 and thus improve the selectivity of NRR [30]. Although ZrO2, as a transition metal oxide, has poor conductivity at room temperature, the empty d-orbital of zirconium atom can obtain electrons from the s-orbital of N2, thereby strengthening metal-N bond and effectively adsorbing N2 [31]. Meanwhile, the electrons of zirconium that have occupied the d-orbital will contribute to the antibonding orbital of N2 to weaken the N N bond and activate N2 [6,32,33]. So, according to the d-orbital theory, Zr is more favorable for N2 adsorption [34]. In addition, Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a type of quasi-zero-dimensional nanomaterials. Compared with traditional carbon-based materials, the synthesis process of CQDs is simple and low-cost [35,36]. Moreover, due to their inherent high conductivity, stability, and high specific surface area, CQDs contribute to improving the catalyst's long-term stability and promoting the sufficient exposure of active sites [37,38]. Therefore, CQDs are mainly used in electrocatalysis, including CRR, ORR, OER, etc. [39–42]. At the same time, during the electrocatalytic reaction process, the coupling of CQDs with suitable metal compounds can effectively regulate the adsorption performance of reaction intermediates, reduce the reaction energy barrier [36], and thus accelerate the catalytic process and improve performance.

In this work, well-dispersed CQDs and x-ZrO2/CQDs-T series catalysts were successfully prepared. The effects of CQDs introduction and calcination temperature on the structure and properties of the catalysts were mainly investigated. The results show that with the addition of CQDs, the FE and NH3 yields of the x-ZrO2/CQDs-T series catalysts are better than those of pure ZrO2 and CQDs. Among them, 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst has the best NRR performance, especially its FE is as high as 66.62 %. This is far superior to other reported NRR catalysts in recent years. The excellent FE of the sample was mainly attributed to: I) The adsorption capacity of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 for N2 was enhanced by the introduction of CQDs, thus promoting N2 molecules to occupy more active sites. II) By weakening the surface alkalinity of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst, thereby inhibiting the binding ability with protons and preventing more protons from occupying active sites. III) The ZrO2/CQDs catalyst effectively and appropriately restricts charge transfer efficiency, thereby effectively limiting electron accessibility and ultimately further weakening the binding to adsorbed protons. Therefore, 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 effectively limits the accessibility of protons/electrons and enhances N2 adsorption capacity, and ultimately effectively suppresses HER reactions to improve the FE of NH3.

Synthesis of CQDs. Citric acid monohydrate (10.9 mmol) and anhydrous ethylenediamine (6.7 mL) were dissolved into deionized water (200 mL) and stirred until clear. Then, the above solution was transferred to a high-pressure reactor and heated for 5 h at 200 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the above-mixed suspension was filtered through a porous diameter anodized aluminum oxide (AAO) membrane and then centrifuged several times with deionized water. The final product is freeze-dried to obtain brown carbon quantum dots.

Synthesis of x-ZrO
2
/CQDs-600. ZrCl4 (1 mmol) and CQDs (0.1 g) are dispersed in deionized water (100 mL) and stirred until homogeneous, then transferred to a reaction vessel. The suspension was heated for 5 h at 200 °C. After natural cooling, the product was washed with deionized water several times and dried at 60 °C for 24 h. The product was then calcined in nitrogen atmosphere at 600 °C for 2 h. Finally, the obtained product was labeled as 1-ZrO2/CQDs-600. The amount of CQDs introduced was optimized without changing the reaction conditions, and the obtained samples were named x-ZrO2/CQDs-600 (where x is the amount of carbon quantum dots added = 0.5 (0.05g), 1 (0.1g), 2 (0.2 g), 3 (0.3g), 4 (0.4g)).

Synthesis of 1-ZrO
2
/CQDs-T. The above prepared 1-ZrO2/CQDs precursors were calcined at high temperatures of 300, 450, 600, 750 and 900 °C, respectively. After natural cooling, the final products were obtained and labeled as 1-ZrO2/CDs-T (where T is calcination temperature = 300, 450, 600, 750, 900 °C).

The synthesis routes of CQDs, x-ZrO2/CQDs-600 and 1-ZrO2/CQDs-T series catalysts are shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, the amounts of CQDs introduced were optimized to obtain a series of catalysts x-ZrO2/CQDs-600. The results showed that 1-ZrO2/CQDs-600 catalyst sample had good NRR activity. On the basis of determining the optimal CQDs introduction, the calcination temperature was then optimized. A series of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-T catalysts were obtained by calcining at different temperatures, and the results showed that the 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst sample had the best NRR activity.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, the XRD spectra of CQDs exhibits a broad peak centered at 2θ = 24°, corresponding to the (002) crystal face of CQDs [43], which indicates the successful synthesis of CQDs. In the x-ZrO2/CQDs-600 series catalysts, the intensity of the CQDs diffraction peak gradually increases with the increase in the amounts of CQDs introduced (Fig. 1b). Moreover, a distinct cubic phase ZrO2 (PDF # No. 49-1642) is observed, and the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 30.1°, 34.9°, 50.2°, 59.7° and 73.9° are derived from the (111), (200), (220), (311) and (400) crystal faces of ZrO2, respectively. From Fig. 1c, it can be observed that in 1-ZrO2/CQDs-T series of catalysts, the intensity of the diffraction peaks of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-T gradually increases with the increase of calcination temperature. When the temperature is gradually higher than 600 °C, the intensity of ZrO2 diffraction peaks is also gradually enhanced, indicating that the crystallinity of ZrO2 is gradually improved.

Fig. 2 shows the morphology of the prepared samples. As shown in Fig. 2a, the prepared CQDs are more uniformly dispersed. Moreover, the particle size of CQDs is more concentrated in 2∼4.5 nm, and its average particle size is 3.48 nm (Fig. 2b). In addition, the HRTEM of CQDs (Fig. 2c) shows that CQDs have a lattice fringe of about 0.33 nm corresponding to (002) crystal face. TEM images of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 (Fig. 2d–e) show that ZrO2 nanoparticles grows attached to CQDs with good dispersion. In addition, the HRTEM of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 (Fig. 2f) reveals that its 0.29 nm lattice fringe corresponds to the (111) crystal face of ZrO2. The introduction of CQDs effectively prevents the aggregation of ZrO2 nanoparticles, which is conducive to the exposure of the active site, thereby improving the NRR.

Fig. S1 shows the full XPS spectra of CQDs and catalyst, and only C, N, and O elements are observed in CQDs. However, the surface of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst contains not only C, N and O elements but also Zr element. Table S1 shows the surface atomic percentages of the samples. Among them, the percentage of N surface atoms in the CQDs is 13.60 %. In comparison, the N atom content of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 surface is significantly reduced to 2.24 %, which is attributed to the elimination of N-containing functional groups of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 under high-temperature calcination. Fig. 3 shows the XPS spectra of CQDs and catalysts. The C1s spectra of different samples show three binding states, corresponding to the typical CC/C–C, C–N and CO [44]. Table 1 shows the specific binding energy values. Fig. 3b shows the N 1s XPS spectra of CQDs and 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750. The N 1s spectra of CQDs shows three kinds of binding energy, which belong to the typical –NH2 (398.5 eV), Pyridinic N (399.0 eV) and C–N (400.7 eV), respectively [45,46]. Meanwhile, according to Table 2 , the relative atomic content of –NH2 accounts for as high as 31.3 % of the overall N content on CQDs surface. However, the N 1s spectra of the 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 sample shows the disappearance of amine groups and only displays two binding energies, corresponding to Pyridinic N and C–N [45,46]. The disappearance of the amine groups is attributed to the high-temperature calcination process. As shown in Table 1, compared with C 1s and N 1s of CQDs, the binding energies of C 1s and N1s of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 samples show a slight positive shift, which indicates that compared to CQDs, the C 1s and N 1s electron cloud densities of the 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 sample are weakened in different degrees.

Fig. 3c shows the O 1s XPS spectra of CQDs, ZrO2, and 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750. The O 1s XPS spectra of CQDs exhibit two binding energies corresponding to C–O–C (531.6 eV) and CO (532.8 eV) [46,47]. The O 1s of ZrO2 sample (Fig. 3c) is deconvoluted into a peak (529.7 eV), which is attributed to lattice oxygen. While the O 1s spectra of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 (Fig. 3c) shows fore binding states, the fore characteristic peaks with binding energies of 529.6 eV, 530.7 eV, 531.4 eV and 532.5 eV are assigned to the typical lattice oxygen (OL), surface oxygen vacancies (OV), C–O–C and CO [46,48,49]. At the same time, according to Table 2, the oxygen vacancy accounts for 19.6 % of the overall oxygen content on 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 surface. On the one hand, the generation of oxygen vacancies may be related to the increase of calcination temperature under anoxic state [50]. On the other hand, the appropriate introduction of CQDs leads to the redistribution of surface charge of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst, which may be due to the fact that lattice oxygen of metal oxides is easily partially reactions with CQDs at high temperatures [51], resulting in the generation of oxygen vacancies. The presence of oxygen vacancies makes ZrO2 have oxygen ion conduction and thus better adsorb and activate N2 [52,53], which is beneficial to improve the NH3 yield of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst. Furthermore, compared with CQDs and ZrO2, the binding energy of O1s in 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 shows a slight negative shift, which indicates an increased electron cloud density around its oxygen (Table 1). Fig. 3d shows the Zr 3d XPS spectra of ZrO2 and 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750. The two apparent peaks at 182.4 eV and 184.8 eV belong to the spin-orbit states (Zr 3d5/2 and Zr 3d3/2) of the Zr 3d spectra, which are assigned to Zr4+ [54]. The many empty d-orbitals of Zr4+ itself can effectively enhance the adsorption of N [30,31], which is beneficial to the improvement of FE for NRR. In addition, to further prove the state and existence of oxygen vacancies, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) tests were performed on ZrO2 and 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalysts. As shown in Fig. 3e, the pure ZrO2 sample did not show obvious paramagnetic resonance, indicating that there was no oxygen defect in pure ZrO2 under strict preservation. In contrast, 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 exhibits obvious paramagnetic resonance at g = 2.002, which proves the existence of oxygen defects on the surface of the sample [55]. The ERP results further confirmed the generation of oxygen vacancies in the 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst, which was consistent with the XPS analysis results. It can be seen from the above that the synthesized CQDs have some oxygen-containing and amine functional groups. And with the increase of temperature, the basic functional group (amine groups) of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 is almost completely eliminated. Meanwhile, the interaction between ZrO2 with surface oxygen vacancies and CQDs leads to the redistribution of charges on the surface of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst.

The NRR catalytic activity of catalysts was evaluated using an H-type electrolytic cell (Fig. S2). The LSV tests were performed in an electrolyte with high purity N2 or Ar saturated 0.1 M Na2SO4 at a scan rate of 5.0 mV s−1. As shown in Fig. 4 a and Fig. S3, for the fore typical catalysts (CQDs, ZrO2, 1-ZrO2/CQDs-600 and 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750), when potentials are less than −0.2 V vs. RHE, the current densities in N2-saturated 0.1 M Na2SO4 are obviously higher than those in Ar. It indicates that they have potentialities to catalyze NRR. In addition, the current densities in N2-saturated 0.1 M Na2SO4 follow the trend 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 > 1-ZrO2/CQDs-600 > CQDs > ZrO2，which demonstrates that the introduction of CQDs and the regulation of temperature may enhance the NRR performance of ZrO2/CQDs catalysts. And compared with 1-ZrO2/CQDs-600, 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 has higher performance, which is consistent with the results of NRR series test. In addition, to study the catalytic activity and optimal working voltage of catalysts for NRR, chronoamperometry (i-t) tests were performed at voltages of −0.2∼-0.6 V vs. RHE. Fig. 4b and Fig. S4-5 show the corresponding i-t curves of different catalysts under different application voltages. It can be seen from the i-t curves that all catalysts have a significant current density at the initial stage of the reaction, and then the current density gradually decreases and becomes stable in a short time. This is mainly because the i-t curves reflect the change of charge concentration gradient near the catalyst surface, and proton mass transfer is primarily carried out by diffusion. However, over time, the double-layer charging and the local concentrations of H+ and N2 near the electrode surface decrease, and the current density tends to a steady state after reaching chemical equilibrium [56]. Furthermore, when the x-ZrO2/CQDs-T series electrocatalysts reach a chemically stable state, the current density will increase with the increase of voltage, which is mainly because when the applied voltage increases, it causes more charge to pass through the electrode surface, resulting in increase in the current density. Fig. 4b shows that the current density of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 remains stable at voltages of −0.2 to −0.6 V vs. RHE, indicating the catalyst has good chemical stability during NRR testing. After NRR i-t test for 3 h, the quantitatively generated NH3 in the electrolyte was collected using the indophenol blue method [57]. Then, the absorbance of collected NH3 was measured by UV–Vis. Finally, the NH3 yield was calculated using the calibration curve (Fig. S6) and Equation S5. Fig. S7-8 show the corresponding UV–Vis spectra of catalysts, and the calculated NH3 yield and corresponding FE are displayed in Fig. 4c and Figs. S9–10. The specific values of FE and NH3 yield can be seen in Table S2 and Table S3. Both NH3 yield and FE are determined by taking the average of three independent tests. Obviously, when the applied voltage moves negatively from −0.2 to −0.6 V, the NH3 yield and FE of all electrocatalysts almost follow the volcano-like changing order. Among them, when the applied voltage is −0.4 V vs. RHE, the NH3 yield and FE of x-ZrO2/CQDs-T series catalysts both reach the highest. However, when the applied voltage is gradually more negative, the NH3 yield and FE of x-ZrO2/CQDs-T catalysts drop sharply, which is mainly because HER competitive reaction has faster reaction kinetics compared to NRR reaction at lower voltage [58,59]. This leads to the adsorption of hydrogen species exceeding nitrogen on the electrode surface, and the active sites are occupied by more H+ [58,59], which hinders the further adsorption and reduction of N2, so the competing reaction HER gradually strengthens to become dominant, eventually leading to decreased NRR activity of the catalysts at lower voltages.

As shown in Fig. 4d and Table 3 , compared with CQDs and ZrO2, the FEs and NH3 yields of x-ZrO2/CQDs-T catalysts are higher. Among x-ZrO2/CQDs-600 series catalysts, with the increase in the amounts of introduced CQDs, the FEs and NH3 yields of x-ZrO2/CQDs-600 series catalysts gradually decrease. When the amount of introduced CQDs is 0.1 g, the NRR performance of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-600 catalyst is the best (FE: 52.82 %, NH3: yield 12.77 μg h−1 mg−1 cat.). In 1-ZrO2/CQDs-T series catalysts, with the gradual increase of calcination temperatures, FEs and NH3 yields of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-T series catalysts increase and then decrease. When the calcination temperature is 750 °C, the NRR performance of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst is the best, its FE is as high as 66.62 %, and the ammonia yield is 15.10 μg h−1 mg−1 cat.. Compared with most reported NRR electrocatalysts in recent years, 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 exhibits more excellent and efficient FE (Table S4). For example, FeSA-N-C (ammonia yield: 7.48 μg h−1 mg−1 cat., FE: 56.55 %) [60], Defective TiO2 (ammonia yield: 7.6 μg h−1 mg−1 cat., FE: 9.17 %) [61], Cu–CeO2 (ammonia yield: 8.1 μg h−1 mg−1 cat., FE: 19.1 %) [62].

In addition, besides ammonia detection, using the Watt-Chrisp method to detect the possible by-product hydrazine (N2H4) in the electrolyte [63], and its standard curve and absorbance curve can be seen in Figs. S11a–b. The test results show that trace amounts of N2H4 are detected at partially applied potentials (Fig. 4e). The highest yield of N2H4 is 0.03 μg h−1mg−1 cat. at −0.2 V vs. RHE, which indicates that 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst could lead to very weak side reaction to N2H4, and it also proves that 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 have excellent selectivity for electrocatalytic ammonia synthesis. To further determine the source of ammonia production and eliminate the possibility of contamination, we conducted 15N2 isotope labeling experiments. 15N2 gas is used as the feed gas to clarify the source of ammonia. As shown in Fig. 4f, the isotope labeling experiment and 1H NMR spectrum indicates that when 15N2 is used as the feed gas for NRR, a doublet coupling peak at 7.2 ppm (J = 72 Hz) assigned to 15NH4 is clearly detected. When 14N2 is used as the feed gas for NRR, a triplet coupling peak (J = 52 Hz) assigned to 14NH4 is detected. This further indicates that NH3 produced by this reaction comes from the electrochemical reduction of N2 rather than external pollution. In addition, we conducted a series of control experiments to further confirm the source of ammonia. As shown in Fig. S12a, the control experiments include: (1) i-t test of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 in N2 saturated electrolyte at open circuit potential (OCP); (2) i-t test of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 in Ar saturated electrolyte at −0.4 V vs. RHE; (3) i-t test of the blank CP in N2 saturated electrolyte at −0.4 V vs. RHE. The obtained UV–Vis spectra show similar signals to those in 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte (Fig. S12b). The calculation results show that the highest NH3 yield is less than 0.02 μg h−1 mg−1 cat. (Fig. S12c). In sharp contrast, 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 produces a large amount of NH3 under ambient conditions, confirming that the detected NH3 only comes from the electrochemical NRR reaction. At the same time, it also shows that 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 sample has superior FE and NH3 yield in 0.1 M Na2SO4. In addition, to evaluate the durability and stability of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst for NRR, we performed i-t tests for 24 h continuously (Fig. S13a). The results show that the current density is almost constant at −0.4 V vs. RHE, indicating that 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 has good stability. At the same time, to further test its durability, the electrolyte was replaced every 3 h at −0.4 V without changing the working electrode, and five consecutive cycle tests were performed. The results show no apparent current density change during the measurement (Fig. 5 a). The corresponding UV–Vis absorption spectra are basically unchanged during five consecutive cycles (Fig. S13b). Meanwhile, there is no significant fluctuations in the NH3 yield and FE (Fig. 5b), and the loss rates of NH3 yield rate and FE are 7.21 % and 6.03 %, respectively. In addition, in order to further confirm the high stability of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 for NRR reaction, the sample after the cycle stability test were characterized by XRD, TEM, XPS and ECSA. The results show that the crystalline phase, morphology and zirconium chemical state of the 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst did not change significantly after the cycle stability test (Figs. S14-S16). At the same time, compared with the ECSA (15.63) of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 material that has not been tested by NRR, the ECSA (150.21) of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 after long-term NRR testing decreased slightly (Fig. S17), but the change was not significant. Therefore, these results also further verify the high stability of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 for NRR reaction.

Some studies have pointed out that ZrO2 itself has certain NRR catalytic activity, and it prefers to bind to N compared to H+ [30,56]. Meanwhile, ZrO2 with oxygen vacancies (Fig. 3e) can better adsorb and activate N2 [34,64,65]. This work finds that compared with pure CQDs and ZrO2, the appropriate introduction of CQDs can significantly improve the FE and NH3 yield of ZrO2/CQDs materials. Especially 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst has the best NRR performance, with an FE of 66.62 % and an ammonia yield of 15.10 μg h−1 mg−1 cat.. In order to further find out the reasons for the increase of NH3 yield of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst, firstly, we evaluated the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) by measuring the electric double-layer capacitance (Cdl) to investigate the effect of ECSA on the NH3 yield. Fig. 5c–d show the capacitive currents at different scan rates obtained from the CV curves (Figs. S18–19). The slope of the fitted line determines the Cdl value, and the specific value can be seen in Table 4 . It can be seen from Fig. 5c that in the x-ZrO2/CQDs-600 series catalysts, the ECSA of x-ZrO2/CQDs-600 gradually decrease with the increased amounts of introduced CQDs. Among them, the ECSA of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-600 catalyst is the highest (14.08). Similarly, in the 1-ZrO2/CQDs-T series catalysts, with the gradual increase of the calcination temperatures, the ECSA of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-T series catalysts show a trend of first increasing and then decreasing. Among them, 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 has the highest ECSA (15.63), which is better than other catalysts. Moreover, the ECSAs of x-ZrO2/CQDs-T series catalysts are higher than those of pure CQDs and ZrO2, indicating that the introduction of CQDs can effectively improve ECSA. The increase in ECSA contributes to the exposure of more active sites [66]. Therefore, more exposure of zirconium sites and oxygen vacancy sites of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst helps to accelerate the adsorption/activation of N2, thereby increasing the NH3 yield. Moreover, compared with H adsorption, zirconium sites are more likely to bind nitrogen [30,31]. The above results indicate that the appropriate introduction of CQDs positively affected the increase of ECSA, and more exposure to active sites contributes to the increase of NH3 yield.

At the same time, in order to find out the reason for the high-efficiency FE of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst. We texted electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to explore the effect of charge transfer resistance on FE (Fig. 5e–f). The specific values of charge transfer resistance (Rct) can be seen in Table 4. According to the kinetic analysis of NRR and HER (Equations 1 and 3), On the one hand, the adsorption of N2 is independent of protons and charges. In contrast, about HER reaction, the adsorption of *H involves electrons [58,59]. On the other hand, NRR is a six-electron reaction, while HER is a two-electron transfer, which causes the reaction kinetics of HER to increase exponentially and be higher than that of NRR by applying a more negative voltage. Therefore, more negative voltage will cause more protons to be adsorbed on the catalyst surface and occupy more active sites, thereby reducing the NH3 yield and FE of NRR. However, from the thermodynamic analysis, the NRR reaction actually requires a more negative potential to weaken the stable N N bond (940.95 kJ mol−1). Therefore, it is very important for the catalyst to effectively inhibit HER reaction without affecting the performance of NRR. However, NRR process involves proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET) process (Equation (2)). Therefore, if the proton/electron restriction of the catalyst is too strong, it will not only inhibit HER but also seriously affect the NRR reaction. On the contrary, if the proton/electron restriction of the catalyst is too weak, then a more negative voltage will lead to a dominant HER reaction. Therefore, when considering the reaction balance between NRR selectivity and HER reaction, the impact of effectively and appropriately limiting protons/electrons on HER and NRR should be fully considered, thereby suppressing the kinetically preferred HER process without affecting the NH3 synthesis process [52], which contributes to the improvement of N2 conversion efficiency and FE of NH3. And without considering the influence of the catalyst, the proton transfer rate is almost identical under the same test conditions [59], so the charge transfer rate is particularly important. The EIS test results shows that CQDs have the fastest charge transfer rate (Rct = 0.762 Ω). Due to the room-temperature semiconductor characteristics of ZrO2, the charge transfer efficiency of ZrO2 is the slowest (Rct = 4.028 Ω). The x-ZrO2/CQDs-T series catalysts show a more efficient charge transfer efficiency than pure ZrO2 and are much lower than CQDs, which means that the charge transfer efficiency of x-ZrO2/CQDs-T is between pure ZrO2 and CQDs. This is mainly due to the fact that CQDs itself has a higher electrical conductivity, and the introduction of CQDs can effectively enhance the intrinsic electrical conductivity of ZrO2/CQDs catalysts [67]. At the same time, the EIS test results also verify this point from the side (charge transfer efficiency from high to low: CQDs > x-ZrO2/CQDs-T > ZrO2). However, charge transfer rate that is too fast will lead to higher HER performance, and charge transfer rate that is too low will lead to a lower FE of NRR. Therefore, the balance between NRR selectivity and charge transfer rate should be fully considered. Due to the introduction of highly conductive CQDs, the charge transfer rate of x-ZrO2/CQDs-T is between pure ZrO2 and CQDs, and this suitable and effective charge limitation can effectively suppress HER without affecting the FE of NRR. This is consistent with the experimental results. In addition, with the increase in the amounts of introduced CQDs, the charge transfer rates of x-ZrO2/CQDs-600 series catalysts gradually increase. And with the increase of calcination temperatures, the charge transfer resistances of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-T series catalysts increase and then decrease. Meanwhile, among the series of x-ZrO2/CQDs-T catalysts, 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 has a lower charge transfer efficiency (Rct = 2.066 Ω), which indicates that compared to other x-ZrO2/CQDs-T catalysts, 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 effectively limits the accessibility of electrons, thus suppressing HER and enhancing FE of NRR. The above results indicate that proper and effective control of the charge transfer rate can effectively weaken the binding of electrons to adsorbed protons, thereby inhibiting the kinetically preferred HER process without affecting the NH3 synthesis process [58], which contributes to the improvement NH3 yield and FE of NRR.

In order to further investigate the reason for the high FE of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst, we performed CO2-TPD tests. In general, according to CO2 desorption temperature, Lewis bases are divided into weakly alkaline (<200 °C), moderately alkaline (200–450 °C) and strongly alkaline (450–600 °C). As shown in Fig. 6 a, CQDs, ZrO2 and 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 exhibit different CO2 desorption peaks. Among them, CQDs exhibit a broad CO2 desorption peak at 183 °C, and ZrO2 presents two desorption peaks at 127 °C and 172 °C. All of them belong to weak base peaks. However, 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 has an obvious CO2 desorption peak at lower temperature (92 °C), which indicates that 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 is weakly alkaline compared to CQDs and ZrO2. This indicates that the appropriate introduction of CQDs can reduce the surface alkalinity of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750, which can effectively weaken the combination of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst with H to limit the accessibility of protons, thereby reducing the HER competition reaction and finally effectively improving FE of NRR. According to XPS analysis (Fig. 3b), in the overall N content of CQDs, –NH2 accounts for as high as 31.3 % (Table 2), and the increase of calcination temperatures leads to the complete decomposition of basic amino functional groups. Therefore, the weakening basicity of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 is probably related to the elimination of the amine functional groups attached to the CQDs. To this end, we further performed FT-IR tests. As shown in Fig. 6b, for ZrO2, the absorption peak at 744.53 cm−1 is mainly attributed to the stretching vibration of Zr–O–Zr bonds [68,69]. The absorption peak at 658.07 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of Zr–O bond [70]. The FTIR spectra of CQDs show that the absorption peak centered at 2910 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–H groups [68,69]. The strong absorption peaks at 1649 cm−1 and 1526 cm−1 are assigned to the CO stretching vibration and the N–H in-plane stretching vibration of the amine group, respectively [46,70,71]. An absorption peak is associated with the C–N stretching vibration at 1353 cm−1 [72]. This indicates that the surface of CQDs contains oxygen functional groups and alkaline amine functional groups, which is consistent with the XPS analysis results (Fig. 3a–c). The absorption peaks at 1148, 1046, and 853 cm−1 show the stretching vibrations of the C–C and C–H groups [72]. The FTIR spectra of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 shows that the N–H absorption peak of the amine group disappears, which is also consistent with the XPS analysis (Fig. 3b). The decomposition of the alkaline functional group (-NH2) also affects the surface acidity and alkalinity of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750, thereby reducing the surface alkalinity of the catalyst.

In addition, N2 adsorption is the first step of the NRR mechanism, so further enhancing N2 adsorption can promote more N2 molecules to occupy the active sites, thereby accelerating the NRR catalytic process and improving FE. To this end, the N2-TPD test was performed. Fig. 6c shows that CQDs have an N2 desorption peak at 215 °C, and the desorption peak temperature of pure ZrO2 is 326 °C. Three distinct peaks of N2 desorption are observed in 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750, which is found to be 213 °C, 337 °C and 455 °C, respectively. Furthermore, compared to CQDs and ZrO2, the amount of N2 desorption from 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 composite is greater, and the temperature is higher. These results show that properly introducing CQDs improves the catalyst's ability to adsorb N2, effectively promoting efficient and stable N2 adsorption on the surface of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst, and preventing excess H from occupying active sites, thus improving FE of NH3.

It can be seen from the above that compared with ZrO2 and CQDs, the introduction of CQDs can effectively improve the FE and NH3 yields of the overall x-ZrO2/CQDs-T catalysts. However, in the x-ZrO2/CQDs-600 series samples, the excessive addition of CQDs also led to a gradual decrease in the FE and NH3 yields. Moreover, with the gradual increase of calcination temperature, the FE and NH3 yields of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-T series catalysts show a trend of first increasing and then decreasing. Among them, 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 had the best NRR performance (FE: 66.62 %, ammonia yield: 15.10 μg h−1 mg−1 cat.). And, the higher FE of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 is mainly attributed to: (I) The adsorption capacity of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 for N2 was obviously improved by the introduction of CQDs, which is beneficial for N2 to occupy more active sites and accelerate the NRR reaction process. (II) High-temperature calcination effectively eliminates the alkaline groups attached to CQDs and weakens the surface alkalinity of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst, which effectively weakens the catalyst's hydrogen adsorption and prevents excessive H from occupying the active sites, reducing the accessibility of protons. (III) Suitable and efficient limitation of the charge transfer rate effectively weakens the combination of charges and adsorbed protons and reduces the accessibility of charges. Thus, the 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst itself effectively suppresses the charge/proton accessibility while enhancing the adsorption of N2 molecules. So, 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst has excellent and efficient FE of NH3. Meanwhile, by optimizing the introduction of CQDs and calcination temperature, the ECSA of the series of x-ZrO2/CQDs-T catalysts is effectively improved, and the improvement of ECSA helps the catalyst to expose more active sites (zirconium sites and oxygen vacancy sites) [66]. More exposure of oxygen vacancies contributes to N2 adsorption/activation [34,64,65]. And compared with H adsorption, zirconium sites are more likely to bind to nitrogen [30,31]. Therefore, more exposure to active sites contributes to improving NH3 yield.

In summary, we used a simple hydrothermal method to synthesize uniformly dispersed CQDs. Through the optimization of CQDs introduction and calcination temperature, the 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 electrocatalyst with high NRR performance was successfully prepared, its FE was as high as 66.62 %, and the ammonia yield was 15.10 μg h−1 mg−1 cat.. The high-efficiency FE of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 is mainly attributed to: I) The adsorption capacity of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 for N2 was significantly enhanced by the proper introduction of CQDs, which further promotes N2 to occupy more active sites and enhances NRR catalytic proceeding. II) High-temperature calcination eliminates the alkaline groups of CQDs, which weakens the surface alkalinity of 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 and weakens the combination with hydrogen, ultimately reducing proton accessibility. III) It is suitable for effectively limiting the charge transfer rate and thereby reducing the accessibility of the charges. Therefore, 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 not only effectively suppresses the competing HER reaction by restricting the accessibility of protons and electrons but also promotes efficient N2 adsorption, leading to the occupation of more active sites and accelerated NRR catalysis. At the same time, the proper introduction of CQDs and the optimization of calcination temperature can effectively improve the ECSA of ZrO2/CQDs and promote the exposure of more active sites (oxygen vacancies and Zr sites) on the surface of the 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst, thereby accelerating the adsorption/activation of N2, ultimately increasing the NH3 yield. Moreover, the 1-ZrO2/CQDs-750 catalyst exhibites excellent stability and durability in electrochemical reactions. These results further reveal the great potential of zirconium-based catalysts for NRR and open new avenues for developing more rationally designed NRR catalysts. However, some efforts are still needed to promote the solubility of N2 in the electrolyte and the desorption of ammonia molecules, and addressing these issues will further improve the NRR performance.
